s: Second-largest monument to a writer on Earth — 287 steps up a Gothic rocket ship
w: Sixty-four statuettes of Scott novel characters ring this Gothic rocket. At 61 metres it is the second-largest monument to a writer in the world, after the Jose Marti column in Havana. You can climb 287 steps for the view. Ink-black sandstone. Completed 1846 by public subscription.
_facts: Second-largest monument to a writer in the world
64 niched statuettes of Scott novel characters
61 metres tall, 287 steps to climb
Completed 1846, funded by public subscription
Waverley station nearby named after Scott's Waverley novels

s: Edinburgh's 'Disgrace' — a Parthenon started in 1822, abandoned half-built
w: Twelve columns is all Scotland got. Intended as a Parthenon replica honouring Scots killed in the Napoleonic Wars, the project ran out of money in 1829 after just this colonnade. Locals called it 'Edinburgh's Disgrace'. Everyone else calls it an accidental masterpiece. Still unfinished. Still magnificent.
_facts: Intended as a full-scale replica of the Parthenon in Athens
Begun 1822, abandoned 1829 when funds ran out
National memorial to Scots killed in Napoleonic Wars
Nicknamed 'Edinburgh's Disgrace' by some locals
On Calton Hill, part of Edinburgh's UNESCO World Heritage Site

s: Nineteen hidden arches under the Royal Mile — once a lawless city below the city
w: When South Bridge opened in 1788 its arches were meant for workshops and taverns. They flooded, the homeless moved in, for decades the vaults ran whisky stills and gambling dens. Sealed up and rediscovered in the 1980s. Britain's most-toured ghost venue — though Burke and Hare almost certainly never stored anyone here.
_facts: South Bridge completed 1788, nineteen arches forming the vaults
Vaults flooded quickly, occupied by homeless then criminals
Ran illegal gambling dens and whisky distilleries
Rediscovered in the 1980s, now ghost and history tours
No solid evidence Burke and Hare used the vaults

s: Where Scotland signed its soul away — and where the dead reputedly get loud
w: The National Covenant of 1638 was signed here, some reportedly in their own blood. In 1679 the kirkyard became a prison for 1,200 Covenanters; hundreds died through a bitter winter. Adam Smith and Allan Ramsay are interred here. The Covenanters' Prison is reputedly the most haunted site in Europe.
_facts: National Covenant of 1638 signed here, some reportedly in their own blood
1,200 Covenanters imprisoned in the open-air kirkyard in 1679
Adam Smith, Allan Ramsay, William Adam buried here
Covenanters' Prison reputedly one of Europe's most haunted sites
Burials have taken place here since the late 16th century

s: Named for the real Jekyll and Hyde — cabinet-maker by day, burglar by night
w: William Brodie sat on Edinburgh council and ran a Lawnmarket workshop, spending his nights robbing the same clients whose locks he had copied in wax. Hanged in 1788 — on a gallows some say he had himself designed. His double life became the seed of Stevenson's Jekyll and Hyde. The tavern stands on his ground.
_facts: William Brodie was a deacon of a trades guild and Edinburgh councillor
Burgled clients by copying their keys in wax
Executed in 1788 on the Edinburgh gallows
His double life inspired Stevenson's Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde
The pub stands near his original Lawnmarket premises

s: An upturned telescope on a hill, dropping a time ball at 1pm since 1852
w: Built 1807-1816 as an upturned telescope to honour Nelson after Trafalgar. In 1852 a time ball was fitted: at one o'clock precisely it drops, synchronised with the One O'Clock Gun from Edinburgh Castle, giving Leith ships a time fix for their chronometers. It still drops. Every weekday.
_facts: Built 1807-1816 to commemorate Nelson's victory and death at Trafalgar
Tower design resembles an upturned telescope
Time ball added in 1852 as a maritime time signal
Synchronised with the One O'Clock Gun from Edinburgh Castle
Restored in 2009

s: Hume, martyrs, and the only Civil War memorial on European soil
w: Opened 1718 on the slopes of Calton Hill. David Hume lies here, his round tomb designed to deter body-snatchers. The tall obelisk honours reformers transported to Australia in the 1790s. And in the corner: the only memorial to Scottish-American Civil War soldiers on European soil.
_facts: Opened 1718 on Calton Hill
David Hume's round tomb designed to deter body-snatchers
Political Martyrs' Monument for reformers exiled to Australia 1793-4
Scotland's American Civil War Memorial erected 1893
Archibald Constable and John Playfair also buried here

s: Leith's 600-year-old mariners' guild — almshouse, court, maritime museum
w: The Masters and Mariners of Leith were incorporated around 1380. The 1816 building served as guild hall, sea-disputes court, almshouse for retired sailors, and treasury for widows. Category A listed, in state care. Maritime instruments, model ships, pilot portraits. Most Leith visitors miss it entirely.
_facts: Mariners' guild founded in Leith around 1380
Current building dates from 1816 by Thomas Brown
Functions included guild hall, maritime court, almshouse and hospital
Category A listed building, now a maritime museum
Held in state care by Historic Environment Scotland

s: The Royal Mile's finest steeple — once a tax office, court and debtors' prison
w: Built in 1591 as the heart of the Canongate — then a separate burgh outside Edinburgh's walls. The corbelled clock tower with its French chateau flourish defines the lower Royal Mile. Now The People's Story Museum on working-class Edinburgh life. Category A listed.
_facts: Built 1591 as administrative and judicial centre of the Canongate
Canongate was a separate burgh, outside Edinburgh's walls, until 1856
French Gothic corbelled clock tower is a Royal Mile landmark
Category A listed building
Now houses The People's Story Museum on working-class Edinburgh life

s: Scotland's oldest concert hall, tucked down the Cowgate since 1763
w: Designed by Robert Mylne for the Edinburgh Musical Society, this elliptical room was the first purpose-built concert hall in Scotland (1763). Later a chapel, Freemasons' lodge, school. Restored by the University of Edinburgh and reopened 2017 with a museum of historic keyboard instruments.
_facts: Designed by Robert Mylne, opened 1763
First purpose-built concert hall in Scotland
Became Baptist chapel, Freemasons' lodge and school over the centuries
Restored by University of Edinburgh, reopened 2017
Houses museum collection of historic keyboard instruments

s: An open-air poem library underfoot — Gaelic, Scots, Latin, English all in stone
w: Lady Stair's Close, beside the Writers' Museum. The paving slabs carry quotations from Scottish literature in all four languages Scotland has written in: Gaelic, Scots, English, Latin. 'Makar' is the Scots word for poet. New stones are added over time. Most visitors walk straight over them. Stop. Read the ground.
_facts: Part of Lady Stair's Close, beside the Writers' Museum
Paving inscribed with Scottish literary quotations in four languages
'Makar' is the Scots word for poet
Described as an evolving national literary monument
New slabs added over time as new writers are honoured

s: A Victorian projector throwing Edinburgh's streets onto a dish in the dark
w: Maria Theresa Short set up the original camera obscura on Calton Hill in 1835 then moved it to the Outlook Tower. A rotating periscope throws a live image of the streets onto a dish in a darkened room — passers-by have no idea. The oldest purpose-built tourist attraction in Edinburgh. Still operating.
_facts: Founded by entrepreneur Maria Theresa Short in 1835
Originally on Calton Hill, later moved to Outlook Tower on Castlehill
Rotating mirror and lens project live street scenes onto a dish
Outlook Tower has been a museum since the late 1890s
One of the oldest continuously operating tourist attractions in Edinburgh

s: The panic wall Edinburgh built after 10,000 Scots died at Flodden in 1513
w: After catastrophic defeat at Flodden Field in 1513, Edinburgh feared invasion and rushed to encircle itself in stone. The best-preserved section survives at the Vennel, a narrow stair off Lauriston Place — substantial 16th-century masonry above the Grassmarket. The city boundary for 200 years.
_facts: Built after the Battle of Flodden in 1513, in which around 10,000 Scots died
Edinburgh feared English reprisal and rushed to build defences
Best-preserved section at the Vennel, off Lauriston Place
Served as Edinburgh's city boundary for roughly 200 years
Extended by the Telfer Wall in the early 17th century

s: The old vet school turned art labyrinth — home to the Royal Dick Bar
w: For most of the 20th century this was the Royal (Dick) Veterinary College. Since 2011: theatre, cinema, studios, microbreweries, and the Dissection Room — a venue that is exactly what it sounds like, tiled and drained. The basement bar keeps the old name. Blissfully odd outside Fringe season.
_facts: Formerly the Royal (Dick) School of Veterinary Studies, University of Edinburgh
Converted into an arts complex in 2011
Major Edinburgh Festival Fringe venue for theatre, visual art and music
Contains working microbreweries and the Dissection Room performance space
Year-round home to artist studios and arts organisations

s: Adam Smith and the grave Robert Burns paid for himself
w: Adam Smith is buried here; so is Robert Fergusson, the poet who moved Burns so deeply that Burns paid for his gravestone and wrote the inscription himself. The kirkyard sits beside Canongate Kirk (1688), parish church of the separate burgh. The royal family still worship here on Edinburgh visits.
_facts: Adam Smith buried here; grave re-signed and landscaped in recent years
Robert Fergusson's gravestone paid for by Robert Burns
Burns wrote the inscription for Fergusson's stone
Canongate Kirk dates from 1688
Royal family worship here during Edinburgh visits; Zara Phillips married here 2011

s: A hidden mill village on the Water of Leith — five minutes from Princes Street
w: Eleven water-driven grain mills once worked this gorge below Dean Bridge. The settlement is 800 years old, known as Water of Leith Village before Edinburgh caught up. The 17th-century granaries survive as flats. Step off a main road and drop 20 metres into another century.
_facts: Grain milling here for over 800 years, at one time eleven working mills
Formerly known as Water of Leith Village
17th-century granary buildings survive, now converted to flats
Below Dean Bridge designed by Thomas Telford, 1832
Part of the Water of Leith Walkway

s: Founded 1128 by David I — open to the sky since the roof came down in 1768
w: David I founded this Augustinian house in 1128 where, legend says, a stag was stopped by a holy rood appearing between its antlers. Charles I was crowned here in 1633. The roof collapsed in 1768 after the Reformation stripped its lead. The ruined nave stands open to the sky inside Holyroodhouse grounds.
_facts: Founded in 1128 by David I of Scotland
Legend: founded where a holy rood appeared between a charging stag's antlers
Charles I crowned in the nave in 1633
Roof stripped after Reformation, collapsed 1768 — a ruin ever since
Scheduled monument within grounds of the Palace of Holyroodhouse

s: Mary Queen of Scots' retreat — where the plot to murder Darnley was hatched
w: Mary Queen of Scots fled here in 1566 after Rizzio's murder. Her nobles drew up the 'Craigmillar Bond' — the conspiracy to kill Lord Darnley — in these walls. He was blown up in Edinburgh three months later. A ruin since the 18th century; quieter and older-feeling than the Castle on the Rock.
_facts: Preston family began building in the late 14th century
Mary Queen of Scots stayed here after Rizzio's murder in 1566
'Craigmillar Bond' conspiracy to kill Lord Darnley reportedly drawn up here
Darnley killed at Kirk o' Field, Edinburgh, in February 1567
Now in care of Historic Environment Scotland as a scheduled monument

s: Victorian Edinburgh's finest necropolis — Playfair, Raeburn, Simpson
w: Opened in 1845, Edinburgh's premier Victorian necropolis. William Henry Playfair, who shaped the 'Athens of the North', lies here; so does Henry Raeburn and James Young Simpson, who introduced chloroform. Unusually well-maintained, competing with Dean Village for Edinburgh's best-hidden corner.
_facts: Opened 1845 by Edinburgh Cemetery Company on the grounds of Dean House
Architect William Henry Playfair buried here
Painter Sir Henry Raeburn and physician James Young Simpson also interred
Lies between the Water of Leith and Queensferry Road
Notable for well-preserved Victorian sandstone monuments

s: Original National Covenant, Bobby's bowl, and a warren of free rooms
w: Huntly House — 16th-century Canongate tenements — holds the city's civic collection: an original National Covenant of 1638, Greyfriars Bobby's collar and bowl, and a reconstruction of Earl Haig's World War One HQ. Centuries of Edinburgh silver, glass and pottery. Free to enter.
_facts: Building dates from around 1570, an amalgamation of Canongate tenements
Holds an original copy of the National Covenant signed at Greyfriars in 1638
Contains Greyfriars Bobby's original collar and feeding bowl
Includes reconstruction of Earl Haig's WWI headquarters
Category A listed building, free admission

s: The Reformer's house — except he almost certainly never lived here
w: The most dramatic overhanging timber frontage on the Royal Mile dates from around 1490 — decades before Knox was born. His link to the building is unverified; a plaque at Warriston Close marks his more likely lodgings. Saved from demolition in the 1840s by the legend. More interesting for what it is not.
_facts: Building dates from around 1490, predating Knox by decades
Knox's residence here is historically unverified
A plaque at Warriston Close marks his more likely actual home
Saved from demolition in the 1840s largely because of the Knox legend
Now attached to the Scottish Storytelling Centre

s: A 1590s courtyard where James VI dined — and a merchant was shot by a schoolboy
w: Baillie John MacMorran hosted James VI and Anne of Denmark here in 1598. Three years before that he had been shot dead stopping a riot at Edinburgh High School — his killer, a schoolboy, was pardoned by the King. Restored by the Scottish Civic Trust. One of the Old Town's finest surviving closes.
_facts: Built by Baillie John MacMorran around 1590
MacMorran hosted James VI and Anne of Denmark here in 1598
MacMorran shot dead in 1595 breaking up a school riot; killer pardoned by King James
Restored by the Scottish Civic Trust
Now used as a hub for adult education

s: Founded 1670 as a physic garden — 13,000 species and the finest glasshouses
w: Physicians Balfour and Sibbald started this as a medicinal herb plot near Holyrood in 1670 — one of the oldest botanic gardens in Britain. Moved to Inverleith in 1820. The Temperate Palm House (1858) is among the tallest glasshouses in the world. Over 13,300 living species. Free to enter.
_facts: Founded 1670 by Andrew Balfour and Robert Sibbald as a physic garden
Moved to the present Inverleith site in 1820
Temperate Palm House (1858) among the tallest glasshouses in the world
Living collection of more than 13,300 plant species
Herbarium holds over 3 million preserved specimens; garden admission free
